narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, close to the usual 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many students would envy. Your Year 1 school decile ranking placed you in the top 10%, though your Year 3 performance dropped to the second decile, around the 20th percentile compared to peers. Coming from a middle-income family and planning full-time studies aligns with the majority of applicants, who are overwhelmingly full-time like you at over 92%. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 40.9% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. Among all factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive sway, pushing the prediction firmly toward bar passage because it's competitively strong. Next in influence was your Year 3 decile ranking, which pulled notably in the opposite direction toward failure risk, likely due to that dip from your earlier high performance. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, again bolstering the case for success with its above-average strength. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA compare favorably to typical students and helped counterbalance concerns, the Year 3 grades stood out as the key drag, tipping the scales against you in this competitive process. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially given the unique challenges Black students like yourself often navigate in rigorous academic paths. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted prep to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps supplementing with bar-focused tutoring to demonstrate upward momentum. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 area could shift your profile dramatically, as even small improvements there carry big weight. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on consistent performance—your strong start shows you have what it takes.
